perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it shall treat all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party with the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information, and the Receiving Party represents that it exercises reasonable care to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to the limitations set forth in this Agreement, this Agreement will inure to the benefit of and be binding upon the parties, their successors and assigns. The Receiving Party agrees that it shall treat all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party with the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information, and the Receiving Party represents that it exercises reasonable care to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The requirement for the receiving party to treat confidential information with reasonable care is moved into the section about agreement terms. This dilutes the legal effect of the 'reasonable care' standard by appearing in the middle of generalized terms.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA), M.G.L. c. 93 § 42
perturbation.law_explanation: MUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy to qualify for trade secret protection. By burying the 'reasonable care' clause within miscellaneous terms, it becomes less prominent, making it harder to prove that the disclosing party took adequate steps to protect their confidential information, potentially undermining a trade secret claim.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha

perturbation.justification: The altered contract text buries the 'reasonable care' requirement within general agreement terms, weakening its legal impact regarding trade secret protection, as per the perturbation explanation. The law explanation asserts that this undermines the ability to prove adequate steps were taken to protect confidential information under MUTSA. Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' as including unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret, which reasonable care is meant to prevent. By obscuring the reasonable care clause, the modified contract reduces the likelihood of successfully proving misappropriation, hence a legal contradiction exists, since proving reasonable care is essential for maintaining trade secret status.

perturbation.original_text: II. This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of the State of Massachusetts and both parties further consent to jurisdiction by the state an d federal courts sitting in Boston , Massachusetts .
perturbation.changed_text: The parties expressly agree that the provision of Information hereunder and discussions held in connection with the Transaction shall not prevent either party from pursuing similar discussions with third parties or obligate either party to continue discussions with the other or to take, continue or forego any action relating to the Transaction . II. This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of the State of Massachusetts and both parties further consent to jurisdiction by the state an d federal courts sitting in Boston , Massachusetts .
perturbation.explanation: Moving the choice of law and jurisdiction clause into the 'No Commitment' section implies that the choice of law is related to the commitment to continue negotiations, potentially creating ambiguity about whether Massachusetts law applies to all aspects of the agreement or only to the 'No Commitment' clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 106, Section 1-301 (Uniform Commercial Code - Choice of Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 106, § 1-301 allows parties to choose the law governing their agreement. By placing the choice of law clause within the 'No Commitment' section, it could be argued that the choice of law only applies to that specific clause, not the entire agreement, potentially creating uncertainty and a basis for challenging the application of Massachusetts law to other parts of the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 106, Article1, Section 1-301 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 1 GENERAL PROVISIONS Section 1-301 Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power To Choose Applicable Law Section 1-301 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 1-301: Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power To Choose Applicable Law Section 1–301. (a) Except as provided hereafter in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to the commonwealth and also to another state or nation, the parties may agree that the law of either the commonwealth or of such other state or nation shall govern the rights and duties under the transaction. Failing to reach such an agreement, this chapter applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to the commonwealth. (b) To the extent that this chapter governs a transaction, if one of the following sections specifies the applicable law, that section governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the section specified: (1) section 2–402; (2) sections 2A–105 and 2A–106; (3) section 4–102; (4) section 4A–507; (5) section 5–116; (6) section 8–110; and (7) sections 9–301 through 9–307. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The modification, by placing the choice of law clause (Massachusetts law) within the 'No Commitment' section, introduces ambiguity as to whether Massachusetts law governs the entire agreement or only that specific section. According to M.G.L. c. 106, § 1-301, parties can agree on the law governing their transaction. However, the placement of the clause raises doubt about the parties' intent for the entire agreement, potentially undermining the clear application of Massachusetts law. The snippet from Section 1-301 (a) indicates that 'the parties may agree that the law of either the commonwealth or of such other state or nation shall govern the rights and duties under the transaction.' The structural flaw weakens the clear intent, creating a potential legal contradiction.
